title: SIGSEGV on podman machine start; no communication with socket
body:

---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. `podman machine start`

**Describe the results you received:**
A SIGSEGV error

**Describe the results you expected:**
the default podman machine starts

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
Interestingly, podman thinks that the machine started successfully (and reports it like this, see the log). If I try to do `podman ps -a` afterwards, nothing happens (it seems to hang until I terminate with CTRL-C). 

I created another machine (just with `podman machine init podman-machine-test`). This machine starts normally. If I then try to do anything (e.g. `podman version`), I get `Error: failed to connect: dial tcp [::1]:54320: connect: connection refused`.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
*it just hangs*
brew says 4.3.0
```

**Output of `podman info`:**

```
*it just hangs*
```

Looks like your podman database is corrupted.
@containers/podman-maintainers Has anyone seen such a stack trace from boltdb before?

Not exactly this, but something vaguely similar (SIGBUS instead of SIGSEGV). We pulled the database, and it was clearly corrupted. Solution was to wipe and start from scratch.

This is now the third instance of Bolt corruption I've heard of in the last year. I'm beginning to become very interested in what's going on.

Thanks for all your answers. If it should really be the case that my database is corrupted, what would be the best course of action? It just seems odd to me that even with another podman virtual machine nothing happens. 

Is there a way to recover the filesystems of the containers within the default-virtual machine?

No real way to recover... Our usual approach is nuke it and start over. Given how rare these cases are, it hasn't been a problem before now. Of course, given the increasing frequency, we may have to reevaluate this.

For clarification, this is the podman database that is corrupted, not necessarily a machine issue. The reason creating another podman machine fails is because your default connection is still on the broken machine. Doing something like a `podman system connection default podman-machine-test` (or removing  the broken machine entirely and creating a new machine) should connect you to your test machine.
Alright, thanks for all the answers - will try to restore from a backup, otherwise I'll just nuke it.
